core_meaning: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える means 'to be visible' or 'to appear/seem' — it describes something that can be seen or that gives a certain impression. Unlike <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る (to look at, an active choice), <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is spontaneous and stative: the thing just comes into view or looks a certain way without effort on your part.

sentences.jp: <ruby>晴<rt>は</rt></ruby>れた<ruby>日<rt>ひ</rt></ruby>には、ここから<ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えるよ。
sentences.translation: On a clear day, you can see Mt. Fuji from here.
sentences.notes: Classic use: something is naturally visible from a location. The subject (<ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>) is marked with が, not を.

sentences.jp: <ruby>霧<rt>きり</rt></ruby>が<ruby>濃<rt>こ</rt></ruby>くて、ほとんど<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えなかった。
sentences.translation: The fog was so thick that I could barely see ahead.
sentences.notes: Shows <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える in the negative past — something was not visible due to external conditions, not a choice.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>は<ruby>年<rt>とし</rt></ruby>より<ruby>若<rt>わか</rt></ruby>く<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える。
sentences.translation: That person looks younger than their age.
sentences.notes: The 'appear/seem' sense: something gives a visual impression. Use an adjective in its く-form or a noun + に before <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>笑顔<rt>えがお</rt></ruby>はどこか<ruby>寂<rt>さび</rt></ruby>しそうに<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えた。
sentences.notes: Literary/written use of the 'appear' sense combined with ～そう, layering two appearance-based expressions.

sentences.jp: このグラフを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ると、<ruby>売<rt>う</rt></ruby>り<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げの<ruby>回復<rt>かいふく</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えてきます。
sentences.translation: Looking at this graph, a recovery in sales is beginning to emerge.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えてくる means something gradually becomes visible or apparent — a common pattern in business or analytical contexts.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える when you mean you actively looked at something — use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る for that. Also don't say テレビを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える; it's always テレビを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る because watching TV is a deliberate action. Be careful not to confuse the 'seem' sense with <ruby>思<rt>おも</rt></ruby>う — <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える implies a visual or surface impression, not an internal opinion.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>られる is the potential form of <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る, meaning 'to be able to see (something)' — it implies capability or opportunity, and typically takes を. <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える, by contrast, means visibility is simply there, with が marking the visible thing. For example, ここから<ruby>海<rt>うみ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える ('the sea is visible from here') emphasizes natural visibility, while ここから<ruby>海<rt>うみ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>られる nudges toward 'you can see/view the sea from here' (opportunity). In everyday speech <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is far more natural for describing what is simply visible.

frequency: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is very common in daily speech, literature, and formal writing alike. The 'visible' sense appears constantly in everyday conversation; the 'appear/seem' sense is slightly more common in written or reflective speech.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える uses が (not を) and no effort — if visibility just happens on its own, or something merely looks a certain way, reach for <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える over <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る or <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>られる.
